The image shows a Pilidiostigma rhytispermum, commonly known as the Small-leaved Plum Myrtle. 

It is a shrub found in wet eucalypt forests and scrubby watercourses. 

It has 5-petalled white flowers that bloom from winter to spring. 

It produces black fruit, up to 10mm in size. 

It prefers moist, well-drained sites with full sunlight to light shade. 

It can grow to be 15 to 20 meters tall and is relatively slow-growing.

description:
The plant in the image is likely a Johannesteijsmannia lanceolata, commonly known as the slender joey palm. Key features include: 

Native to Peninsular Malaysia.

Has large, undivided leaves, up to 3.5m long.

The underside of the leaves is covered in small brown scales.

Flowers smell of sour milk.

It is cultivated in tropical and subtropical regions.

The image shows a plant, likely a bur buttercup, characterized by its small, star-shaped flower and surrounding foliage. 

Helpful information: 

Bur buttercup plants are often found in fields and open areas.

They produce small, spiky burs that can stick to clothing and animal fur.

The plant's leaves are typically lobed or divided.

Bur buttercups may have medicinal properties, but should be used with caution.

It is important to properly identify the plant before using it for any purpose.

The plant in the image is likely an orchid, possibly an Oncidium orchid or a Pyrrosia longifolia fern. Here's some information about it: 

Type:

It is an epiphytic plant, meaning it grows on other plants (like trees) for support but doesn't harm them. 

Leaves:

The plant has long, narrow, fleshy leaves, recurved and can be up to 3.6 cm wide. The upper surface is pale green and smooth, while the lower surface is silvery-grey with a layer of hairs. 

Habitat:

This type of plant is found in regions from Nepal to Hainan and West Malesia. 

Conservation:

It's important to conserve this plant to maintain biodiversity and ecosystem health. 

Location:

Given the user's location in Bukit Panjang, Singapore, it's plausible to find this plant as it is native to the region.
The plant in the image is a Bird's Nest Fern (Asplenium nidus). It is an epiphytic fern native to tropical southeastern Asia, eastern Australia, and other regions.

The image shows a Rain Tree (Samanea saman), also known as a Monkey Pod tree. Key features and information about Rain Trees include: 

Native to northern South America. 

Commonly used as an ornamental and shade tree in parks and gardens. 

Characterized by a wide-spreading crown and large size. 

In Singapore, rain trees are a common feature of the cityscape. 

A notable rain tree is located at Faber Point, planted in 1971 by Acting Prime Minister Goh Keng Swee. 

Heritage trees in Seletar include rain trees, some dating back to the 1930s.

The image shows Ixora coccinea, also known as the jungle geranium or flame of the woods. It is a tropical evergreen shrub with clusters of small, bright orange-red flowers. Key characteristics include: 

Dense, multi-branched shrub, typically 4–6 ft tall but can reach 12 ft.

Rounded form, with a spread that may exceed its height.

Flowers year-round.

Easy to grow and suitable for hedges and topiaries.

Attracts butterflies.

Sweet Ixora flowers are edible and can be used to decorate cakes.

The flower in the image is a Bamboo Orchid, scientifically known as Arundina graminifolia. Key information about this plant: 

It is an evergreen rhizomatous orchid.

It thrives in hardiness zones 10-13.

It prefers warm, low sun to dappled light.

It requires constantly moist soil.

It grows well in orchid compost with mildly acidic to mildly alkaline pH.

It's a terrestrial perennial orchid.

The image shows Allamanda cathartica, also known as golden trumpet or yellow allamanda. It is an evergreen shrub native to Brazil. Key features include: 

It can grow up to 2.5 meters in height.

It is characterized by its bright yellow, trumpet-shaped flowers.

It is commonly cultivated in tropical regions as an ornamental plant.

It attracts pollinators such as bees.

It can host nesting for birds like the Yellow-vented Bulbul.

The plant in the image is likely an Alocasia, also known as elephant ear or giant taro. Key information about this plant includes: 

Native to rainforests of Island Southeast Asia, New Guinea, and Queensland.

Cultivated in the Philippines, Pacific islands, and other tropical regions.

Characterized by large foliage, resembling elephant ears.

Can grow to a height of 1–1.5 meters.

Sap can cause skin irritation and burning if ingested.

Flowers are small, enclosed by a yellowish spathe.

Prefers well-lit situations.

Some varieties can reach up to 2.4 meters.

The image shows a Licuala grandis, also known as the Ruffled Fan Palm or Vanuatu Fan Palm. Key features include: 

Native to the Solomon Islands and Vanuatu.

Known for its large, pleated, fan-shaped leaves.

Prefers lightly shaded or full shade areas.

Green fruits turn red when ripe, containing a single seed inside.

Can grow as a shrub or small tree, reaching up to 15m in height.

Commonly found in tropical locations.

The image shows the trunk of a Diospyros malabarica tree, also known as the Black and White Ebony or Gaub tree. Here's some information about it: 

It naturally grows in rainforests along riverbanks and streams. 

It thrives in full sunlight and fertile, well-drained soil. 

The bark is black, and the ripe fruit is edible. 

The tree's timber is of high quality and is often exported. 

The inner bark and unripe fruit have been used traditionally to treat fevers, diarrhea, and hemorrhage.

description: Pelagodoxa mesocarpa est une espèce de palmier. Il est endémique à Vanuatu, où il est originaire des îles Banks, d'Erromango et du sud-ouest de Malakula. Il a été introduit dans les îles Salomon. en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pelagodoxa_mesocarpa

Actinorhytis calapparia est une espèce de plantes à fleurs la famille des Arecaceae (palmiers). C'est la seule espèce du genre Actinorhytis. Actinorhytis calapparia est répandue en Nouvelle-Guinée et aux Îles Salomon. Elle pousse à l'état naturel dans les forêts tropicales humides, du bord de mer jusqu'à 1 000 mètres d'altitude. fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Actinorhytis_calapparia

Odoardo Beccari a collecté ce palmier pour la première fois en Nouvelle-Guinée en 1872 et l'a classé dans le genre Ptychosperma sous l'épithète micranthum, qui signifie "petite fleur" en latin. Les extrémités des feuilles n'étaient pas dentelées comme celles de la plupart des espèces du genre et, onze ans plus tard, Sir Joseph Hooker, alors directeur des jardins de Kew, l'a transféré à Rhopaloblaste. L'adaptation était imparfaite car les fleurs mâles et les éophylles des semis de Rhopaloblaste ne correspondaient pas. En 1970, le spécialiste des palmiers Harold E. Moore a analysé la collection de Beccari et, bien qu'elle porte une couronne, l'a reclassée dans le genre Heterospathe, qui n'en porte pas. Enfin, en 2006, des tests ADN ont révélé que la plante ne correspondait à aucun genre connu. Le nom de genre fait référence à John Dransfield, en l'honneur au grand nombre de recherches et d'études sur les Arecaceae. en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dransfieldia

Cette plante est originaire d’Indonésie (archipel des Moluques et Irian Jaya), où elle pousse jusqu’à 300 m d’altitude sur de la lave, sur les rives des cours d’eau et les bords des forêts, dans des zones ouvertes et en plein soleil ; elle se comporte comme une plante pionnière colonisant des zones dégradées. Le nom de genre honore le navigateur italien Antonio Pigafetta ; le nom de l’espèce est le mot latin “filaris” = filiforme, filamenteux, peut-être en référence aux fibres minces obtenues à partir des feuilles et utilisées pour faire des vêtements, des tapis, etc www.monaconatureencyclopedia.com/pigafetta-filaris/?lang=fr

Copernicia est un genre de palmiers. Il comprend des espèces appelées communément palmiers à cire. Le genre a été nommé ainsi par le botaniste Carl Friedrich Philipp von Martius, en 1837, en hommage à l'astronome polonais Nicolas Copernic. fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Copernicia

Date: 6 November 2016
Time: 9.57 am
Identity: Lotus flower (Common), Nelumbo nucifera (Scientific)
Location: Marina Bay Sands, In a pond filled with lotus flowers under Art Science Museum
Observations: Small purple flower, above lotus leaf, floating on water

Flower had five twisted petals. The petals were white, but a few parts were yellow(especially on the edge). Had a solid stem that looked like a bamboo stick. Some parts of stem was green, some was yellow.

Growing between the cracks of stairs. Hard to estimate length of each petal but the diameter of the whole white flower was 2~2.5cm. I think the flower was originally a pink/purple flower, however turned into a white flower shown in the picture. The edge of some petals of the flower were slightly thick.
This yellow flower had 8 petals, and the edge of the petal was slightly split.
This flower was very small(each petal was 1cm), and the flower looked like a barley seed. The stem of the flower had some spike looking things.
This plant had big leaves, thick leaves with a hard stem. The leaf size were 22~26cm. The leaf looked like a heart.
Colors of leaf was green and white, but under the leaf was red. Size of leaf was about 4~6cm.

Red color. Top part of the flower looks like a pepper. Flower is very thin and long(looks like a trumpet). White color flowers were also found, but most of them were red. Size of flower was about 3~4cm. Stem was very thin.
Center of flower was yellow, petals of the flower were light pink. Each flower had 5 petals. Each petal was quite thick. Length of the petal was about 3cm.

Orange-Red color petals. Had 8~10 petals, and every petal had a black dot on the edge. Size of every petal was different, but the whole size of a flower(only the flower) was about 12cm. The stem was quite thin, color of stem was light green. Leaves were green.

place_guess: Marina Bay Sands, Marina, Singapore, SG
description: The plant has a very interesting shape. It looks like a flower and has several layers of itself too. It's size is approximately about the size of an average mans hand. It is a very light/lime green plant. When I touched it is was not very thick. It is easily able to rip apart if someone purposely touched. The plant also is on the water which I think means that it grows and lives above the surface of water.

description: A 10-15 foot tall tree planted outside in a bed of several trees. The trunks if the trees were not as thick as trees else where but were Rather skinny, being completely outdone at the massive top. The leaves made of the top of the better. The leaves were dark and light green, the dark more on top. They leaves were also about the size of a horizontal American dollar bill and the length was a good 6 to 8 inches. They took up of a lot of space and were bunched together along the branches of the tree.
Tropical flower with thick white or pink petals and a yellow/orange color on the inside. Long pink flower buds. On tree with long green leaves.
